class_schedule.topic: Writing for Professional Audiences: Principles and Practices
learning_objectives: Analyze how professional writing differs from academic writing in audience, purpose, and conventions
Identify the principles of plain language writing for professional contexts
Understand how document design and information architecture shape reader comprehension
readings: Williams, Style: Ten Lessons in Clarity and Grace, Ch. 1-2
Plain Language Action and Information Network (PLAIN) guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: Professional writing is writing that works. It does not showcase the writer's intelligence — it delivers information the reader needs in the form the reader can use. This course teaches you to write for professional contexts with clarity, precision, and purpose.
key_concepts: Audience analysis for professional writing: what does the reader need, know, and expect?
Plain language principles: active voice, concrete nouns, short sentences, clear structure
Document design: headings, white space, lists, and visual hierarchy
Information architecture: organizing content for findability and usability
The professional writing portfolio: building a body of work for the job market
lecture_notes.demonstration: Revising a badly written corporate memo into clear professional prose, identifying each change and its rationale

review.value: Writing that allows the reader to find what they need, understand what they find, and use what they understand — achieved through clear organization, common words, active voice, and appropriate document design
How a document looks affects how it reads: headings create structure, white space prevents overwhelm, bulleted lists make parallel items scannable, and typography guides the eye through the content

learning_objectives: Write professional reports with clear executive summaries, findings, and recommendations
Draft proposals that identify problems and argue for solutions with supporting evidence
Apply professional email and correspondence conventions
readings: Alred et al., Handbook of Technical Writing, selected entries on reports and proposals
Sample professional reports and proposals (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Reports, proposals, and correspondence are the backbone of professional writing. Each has specific conventions that readers expect, and violating those conventions costs credibility regardless of the quality of your thinking.
key_concepts: The executive summary: the most-read and most-important section of any report
Report structure: purpose, methods, findings, conclusions, recommendations
Proposal structure: problem, proposed solution, qualifications, budget, timeline
Professional email: subject lines, tone, action items, and the one-screen rule
Audience adaptation: writing the same content for executives, peers, and clients
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing three versions of the same project update: an executive summary for leadership, a detailed email for the project team, and a client-facing progress report

review.value: The one section that every reader reads. It must stand alone, contain the most important conclusions and recommendations, and fit on one page. If the reader reads nothing else, the executive summary should be sufficient.
Professional emails should fit on one screen without scrolling. If the message is longer, it should be a report or document attached to a brief email summarizing the key points and requested actions.

class_schedule.topic: Content Strategy and Writing for the Web
learning_objectives: Analyze how web reading behavior differs from print reading behavior
Apply content strategy principles: user-centered content, SEO writing, and information hierarchy
Write web content that is scannable, findable, and accessible
readings: Redish, Letting Go of the Words, Ch. 1-3
Nielsen Norman Group, 'How Users Read on the Web'

lecture_notes.introduction: People do not read on the web — they scan. Web writing that ignores this reality fails, no matter how well-crafted the sentences are. Content strategy puts the user's needs at the center of every writing decision.
key_concepts: F-pattern reading: users scan web pages in an F-shaped pattern, reading less as they go down
Front-loading: the most important information comes first at every level (page, section, paragraph, sentence)
SEO writing: incorporating search terms naturally so content is findable
Accessibility: writing for screen readers, clear link text, alt text for images
Content governance: who creates, reviews, approves, and maintains content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rewriting a print brochure as a web page, applying front-loading, scannable structure, clear headings, and accessible design

review.value: Putting the most important information first — in the page, in the paragraph, in the sentence — because web readers decide within seconds whether content is relevant and will leave if they do not find what they need immediately

Writing web content so that all users can access it, including those using screen readers, those with cognitive disabilities, and those on slow connections — an ethical obligation and often a legal requirement

class_schedule.topic: Editing and Style Guides: Professional Standards
learning_objectives: Apply copyediting techniques to professional documents
Understand the function and structure of organizational style guides
Develop a consistent editorial voice across different document types
readings: Chicago Manual of Style, selected sections on grammar and usage
Sample organizational style guide (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Professional writing demands consistency. Style guides ensure that every document from an organization sounds like it comes from the same voice, follows the same conventions, and meets the same quality standards.
key_concepts: Copyediting vs. developmental editing: sentence-level correction vs. structural revision
Style guide elements: voice and tone, terminology, formatting, grammar preferences
The editorial process: drafting, self-editing, peer review, copyedit, final review
Common professional writing errors: nominalizations, passive voice overuse, jargon, hedging
Building an organizational voice: how a style guide creates consistency across writers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Copyediting exercise: students edit a professional document for consistency, clarity, and adherence to a provided style guide

review.value: An organization's voice is its personality in writing (authoritative, friendly, technical); tone varies by context (the voice stays the same but the tone shifts between a crisis communication and a product launch)

Professional writing is never published without editing. The standard process: the writer drafts and self-edits, a peer reviews for substance and structure, a copyeditor corrects language and consistency, and a final reviewer approves for publication.

learning_objectives: Curate a professional writing portfolio demonstrating range and competence
Write an effective resume and cover letter tailored to specific positions
Present professional writing samples with context and reflection
readings: Resume and cover letter best practices guide

lecture_notes.introduction: A professional writing portfolio is your proof of competence. It demonstrates not just that you can write but that you can write for different audiences, in different genres, and to different purposes — and that you can reflect on your own work critically.
key_concepts: Portfolio curation: selecting samples that demonstrate range, not just quantity
Contextualizing samples: providing the audience, purpose, and constraints for each piece
The resume as professional writing sample: every word and formatting choice matters
Cover letters as arguments: making a case for your fit with a specific position
Digital portfolios: websites, PDFs, and online platforms for showcasing work
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: peer review of portfolio drafts with feedback on selection, presentation, and professional polish

review.value: A portfolio is not a collection of everything you have written — it is a curated selection that demonstrates range, quality, and judgment. Each sample should serve a purpose, and the portfolio as a whole should tell a story about your capabilities.

Your resume is the first piece of professional writing a potential employer sees. Formatting inconsistencies, vague language, and passive constructions undermine your credibility as a writer before anyone reads your portfolio.

questions.question: What are three principles of plain language writing, and why are they important in professional contexts?
questions.answer: Three principles: (1) Use active voice so readers know who is responsible for actions. (2) Use concrete, specific language rather than abstract jargon so readers understand immediately. (3) Organize content with clear headings and front-loaded structure so readers can find what they need. These principles matter because professional readers are busy, scan rather than read linearly, and need to act on what they read.

rubric.key: Three accurate plain language principles identified
Each principle is explained, not just named

questions.question: What is an executive summary, and why is it the most important section of a professional report?
questions.answer: An executive summary is a standalone synopsis of a report's key findings, conclusions, and recommendations, typically one page or less. It is the most important section because many readers — especially decision-makers — will read only the executive summary. It must contain everything a reader needs to understand the report's significance and act on its recommendations without reading the full document.

rubric.key: Explains why it is the most important section
Notes that it must stand alone

questions.question: What is 'front-loading' in web writing, and at what levels of a document should it be applied?
questions.answer: Front-loading means placing the most important information first. It should be applied at every level: the page level (most important content at the top), the section level (key point in the heading and first paragraph), the paragraph level (topic sentence first), and the sentence level (subject and verb before qualifications). This matches F-pattern scanning behavior, ensuring readers encounter critical information even if they read only the beginnings.

rubric.key: Identifies multiple levels where it applies

questions.question: What is the difference between 'voice' and 'tone' in an organizational style guide?
questions.answer: Voice is the organization's consistent personality in writing — it stays the same across all communications (e.g., authoritative, friendly, expert). Tone is the emotional register that varies by context while the voice remains constant. The same organization might use a reassuring tone in a crisis communication and an enthusiastic tone in a product launch, but both should sound like the same organization.

rubric.key: Notes that tone varies by context while voice remains consistent

major_assignments.title: Professional Writing Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Compile a professional writing portfolio containing at least five samples demonstrating range across professional genres: report, proposal, web content, correspondence, and one genre of your choice. Each sample must be accompanied by a context note explaining the audience, purpose, and constraints.
objectives: Demonstrate competence across multiple professional writing genres
Apply plain language, document design, and audience analysis principles
Curate and present professional work with critical reflection
Produce a portfolio suitable for use in job applications
deliverables: Portfolio with at least 5 professional writing samples across different genres
Context note (200 words) for each sample explaining audience, purpose, and constraints
Polished resume and one tailored cover letter
Portfolio introduction (500 words) articulating your professional writing philosophy

criteria: All samples demonstrate clear, professional prose
Audience awareness is evident in every piece
Plain language principles are applied consistently
Portfolio demonstrates range across at least five professional genres
Sample selection shows judgment about quality and relevance
Context notes provide genuine insight into the writing situation
Portfolio tells a coherent story about the writer's capabilities
Portfolio design is professional and consistent
Cover letter makes a compelling argument for a specific position
Introduction articulates a clear professional identity
High-quality professional writing across multiple genres
Strategic curation demonstrating range and judgment
Professional presentation suitable for the job market
Thoughtful context notes and portfolio introduction

portfolio.description: Professional writing portfolio with five samples

weekly_writing_labs.description: In-class writing exercises across professional genres

quizzes.description: Quizzes on professional writing principles and conventions

peer_editing.description: Quality of peer editing and feedback on classmates' work

final_presentation.description: Professional presentation of portfolio to the class

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory, especially for writing labs and peer editing sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Portfolio components penalized 10% per day late. Weekly labs not accepted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer editing is collaborative. All writing samples in the portfolio must be your own individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Submitting work written for another course or by another person is not permitted. AI tools may be used for editing assistance but must be documented.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop required for every class session. Word processing software, web content tools, and LMS access.
